cards.grammarNote:
**Voy a casa** (I'm going home) — notice no article before **casa** in this fixed phrase, unlike other destinations: **voy a la escuela**, **voy al banco**.
**Casa** is feminine: **la casa**. Used for the concept of 'home' as much as the physical building.

**Vivo en** (I live in) uses **en** because you are located there, not travelling there.
Contrast: **Voy a un apartamento** (I'm going to an apartment) vs **Vivo en un apartamento** (I live in an apartment).
Alternative word in Spain: **el piso** (literally 'floor/storey').

**La escuela** is feminine — use **la** (the) and **una** (a).
**Van a la escuela** — going to a place uses **ir a**: **voy a la escuela**, **vamos al mercado**.
**Los niños** = the children (masculine plural covers mixed or all-male groups). Feminine: **las niñas**.
cards.phrase: Los niños van a la escuela.

cards.grammarNote:
**La universidad** is feminine despite ending in **-dad** (all **-dad** nouns are feminine: la ciudad, la libertad, la identidad).
**Estudia en la universidad** — use **en** for being/studying at a location.
The phrase uses **ella** (she) explicitly, which is optional in Spanish since the verb ending already signals the subject.

**La oficina** is feminine — **la** / **una oficina**.
**Trabaja en una oficina** — use **en** for location (working at/in a place).
Compare: **Voy a la oficina** (I'm going to the office — direction) vs **Estoy en la oficina** (I'm at the office — location).
**Él** (he) includes an accent mark to distinguish it from **el** (the).

**a** + **el** → **al** · **de** + **el** → **del** (mandatory contractions)
**Voy al hospital** (not *a el*) · **Vengo del banco** (not *de el*)
**El hospital** from Latin **hospes** (host/guest) — same root as **hotel**, **hostal**, **hostelería**, and English *hospitality*.
**El** hospital — masculine gender.

**Necesito ir al supermercado** — **al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction before a masculine noun).
**La tienda de abarrotes** is the Latin American term; Spain uses **la tienda de comestibles** or simply **el supermercado**.
**Necesito + infinitive** = I need to [do something]: **necesito ir**, **necesito comprar**, **necesito salir**.
cards.englishPhrase: I need to go to the supermarket.

cards.grammarNote:
**Del** = **de + el** — mandatory contraction. **Vengo del banco** (I'm coming from the bank).
**El banco** is masculine. Note: **banco** also means *bench* — context tells them apart.
**Vengo de** = I'm coming from. Compare: **Voy al banco** (going to) vs **Vengo del banco** (coming from).

**La biblioteca** — feminine noun, from Greek **biblion** (book) + **theke** (storage/case). The same root gives English *bibliography* and *Bible*.
**Estudio en la biblioteca** — use **en** for location (being/studying there), not **a**: **en** = at/in, **a** = to (direction).
Don't confuse with **la librería** = bookshop (a *false friend* — librería is NOT library).

**El restaurante** is masculine — **el** / **un restaurante**.
**Está lleno** uses **estar** (not **ser**) because fullness is a temporary state. Compare: **El restaurante es famoso** (permanent quality — it's famous) vs **El restaurante está lleno** (temporary — it's full right now).
**Lleno** agrees in gender/number with the subject: lleno / llena / llenos / llenas.

**El café** is masculine — **el café**, **un café**. The accent on **é** is part of the spelling.
**Nos encontramos en el café** — **nos encontramos** is reflexive (encontrarse = to meet each other). The reflexive pronoun **nos** signals a mutual action.
**En el café** — use **en** for being at a location. Compare: **Vamos al café** (we're going to the café).

**El hotel** is masculine — **el hotel**, **un hotel**.
**Está en el centro** uses **estar** — location is always expressed with **estar**, never **ser**.
**El centro** = the city centre / downtown. Full form: **el centro de la ciudad**.
From Latin **hospes** (host/guest) — same family as **hospital**, **hostal**, **hospedaje**.
cards.phrase: El hotel está en el centro.

cards.grammarNote:
**El aeropuerto** is masculine. Etymology: **aero** (air, from Greek **aer**) + **puerto** (port/harbour) — literally *air port*, matching the English word.
**Puerto** alone = port/harbour: **el puerto de Barcelona**.
**Está lejos de aquí** — location uses **estar**. **Lejos de** = far from; **cerca de** = near.
cards.englishPhrase: The airport is far from here.
cards.phrase: El aeropuerto está lejos de aquí.

cards.grammarNote:
**La estación** is feminine (all **-ción** nouns are feminine: la nación, la canción, la estación).
**Cerca del hotel** — **del** = **de + el** (mandatory contraction). You can't write *de el hotel*.
**Está cerca** — location with **estar**; **cerca de** = near.
cards.englishPhrase: The station is near the hotel.
cards.phrase: La estación está cerca del hotel.

cards.grammarNote:
**La parada** (feminine) = the stop. **Parada de autobús** = bus stop; **parada de metro** = metro stop.
**Espera en la parada** — **Espera** is the informal (tú) imperative of **esperar**. Formal: **Espere**.
**En** here means *at* (being at a location). **Autobús** (Spain) = **bus** (Latin America); also **camión** (Mexico), **colectivo** (Argentina).
cards.phrase: Espera en la parada de autobús.

cards.grammarNote:
**El parque** is masculine — **el parque**, **un parque**.
**Juegan en el parque** — **jugar** (to play a game/sport) uses **en** for location. Note: **jugar** ≠ **tocar**: **jugar al fútbol** (play football) but **tocar la guitarra** (play the guitar).
**Los niños juegan** — stem-changing verb: **juego**, **juegas**, **juega**, **jugamos**, **jugáis**, **juegan** (u→ue except nosotros/vosotros).
cards.englishPhrase: The children play in the park.
cards.phrase: Los niños juegan en el parque.

cards.grammarNote:
**La farmacia** is feminine — **la farmacia**, **una farmacia**.
**¿Hay una farmacia cerca?** — **hay** (from **haber**) = there is / there are. It's invariable: **hay una farmacia**, **hay dos farmacias**.
**Cerca** = nearby (no **de** needed when no specific reference follows). **Cerca de aquí** = near here.

**La plaza** is feminine — common in Spanish-speaking cities: **la Plaza Mayor**, **la plaza del pueblo**.
**Hay una fuente** — **hay** = there is. Never use **está** to say something exists; use **hay**.
**La fuente** = fountain (also means *source* — context clarifies).
cards.englishPhrase: There's a fountain in the square.
cards.phrase: Hay una fuente en la plaza.

cards.grammarNote:
**La calle** is feminine (unusual since most **-e** nouns can go either way — just memorise this one).
**Está en esta calle** — location uses **estar**. **Esta** = this (feminine demonstrative, agrees with **calle**).
**Esta** (no accent) = this (adjective); **ésta** (old spelling with accent) = this one (pronoun — now the accent is optional).
cards.englishPhrase: The restaurant is on this street.
cards.phrase: El restaurante está en esta calle.

cards.grammarNote:
**El paso de peatones** — literally 'the crossing of pedestrians'. **Paso** = step/crossing; **peatón** (pedestrian) → plural **peatones**.
Also called **el paso de cebra** (zebra crossing) in Spain, from the black-and-white stripes.
**Cruza en el paso** — **Cruza** is the informal (tú) imperative of **cruzar**.
cards.phrase: Cruza en el paso de peatones.

cards.grammarNote:
**El cruce** is masculine — from **cruzar** (to cross).
**Gira a la izquierda** — direction phrases: **a la izquierda** (to the left), **a la derecha** (to the right), **todo recto** (straight ahead). These are the three core direction phrases to memorise.
**En el cruce** = at the intersection. **En** marks the specific point where the turn happens.
cards.phrase: Gira a la izquierda en el cruce.

cards.grammarNote:
**El puente** is masculine.
**Cruzamos el puente** — past tense (preterite) of **cruzar** (nosotros form). The preterite ending **-amos** is the same for present and past with -ar verbs for nosotros — context clarifies tense.
**El puente** also colloquially means a long weekend (a 'bridge' between a holiday and the weekend).

**La esquina** is feminine — the corner of a street/building.
**Dobla a la derecha** — **a la derecha** (to the right), **a la izquierda** (to the left). These phrases always use **a + la** because **derecha** and **izquierda** are feminine nouns.
**Doblar** and **girar** both mean 'to turn' when giving directions — both are common.
cards.phrase: Dobla a la derecha en la esquina.

cards.grammarNote:
**El centro** is masculine. Full form: **el centro de la ciudad** (city centre).
**El museo está en el centro** — location uses **estar** + **en**.
**El museo** is also masculine. Many cultural buildings are masculine: **el museo**, **el teatro**, **el estadio**, **el cine**.
cards.phrase: El museo está en el centro.

cards.grammarNote:
**El barrio** is masculine — **el barrio**, **un barrio**.
**Vivo en un barrio tranquilo** — use **en** for residence location. **Tranquilo** (quiet/calm) is an adjective that agrees with the masculine noun: **barrio tranquilo** / **calle tranquila**.
In many cities, neighbourhoods have famous names: **el Barrio Gótico** (Barcelona), **el Barrio de La Boca** (Buenos Aires).
cards.englishPhrase: I live in a quiet neighbourhood.

cards.grammarNote:
**La playa** is feminine — **la playa**, **una playa**.
**La playa es hermosa** — use **ser** for a permanent or inherent quality (beauty of a place). Compare: **La playa está llena** (the beach is crowded — temporary state, usar **estar**).
**Hermosa** (beautiful) is the feminine form; **hermoso** for masculine.

**La montaña** is feminine — **la montaña**, **una montaña**.
**Es muy alta** uses **ser** — height/size is an inherent characteristic of a mountain.
**Alta** is the feminine form of **alto** (tall/high). Adjectives agree in gender and number with the noun they describe.

**El museo** is masculine.
**Quiero visitar el museo** — **querer + infinitive** = to want to do something. **Quiero** is a stem-changing verb (e→ie): quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, queréis, quieren.
**Visitar** (to visit a place) takes a direct object with no preposition: **visitar el museo**, **visitar la ciudad**.
cards.englishPhrase: I want to visit the museum.

cards.grammarNote:
**El cine** is masculine — short for **cinematógrafo**.
**Vamos al cine** — **al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction). You can never write *a el cine*.
**Esta noche** = tonight. **Ir al cine** is the standard phrase for going to the movies — the same as 'going to the cinema' in British English.
cards.englishPhrase: We're going to the cinema tonight.

cards.grammarNote:
**El mapa** is masculine — despite ending in **-a** (which usually signals feminine). Other masculine **-a** nouns: **el problema**, **el programa**, **el tema**, **el día**. Many come from Greek.
**¿Tienes un mapa?** — informal question using **tú** form. Formal: **¿Tiene usted un mapa?**

**La dirección** is feminine (all **-ción** nouns are feminine).
**¿En qué dirección está?** — use **estar** for location. **En qué** = in which / what.
**Dirección** also means *address* (postal address) — context makes it clear: **¿Cuál es tu dirección?** (What's your address?)

**La distancia** is feminine — all **-ancia** nouns are feminine: **la distancia**, **la importancia**, **la instancia**.
**La distancia al hotel** — **al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction).
**¿Cuál es la distancia...?** = What is the distance...? Use **cuál** (not **qué**) before **es** when asking for a specific value from a set.
cards.englishPhrase: What's the distance to the hotel?

cards.grammarNote:
**Ir** is highly irregular. Present tense: **voy**, **vas**, **va**, **vamos**, **vais**, **van**.
**Ir a + place** = to go to a place: **voy al supermercado**, **voy a casa**, **voy a la playa**.
**Ir a + infinitive** also expresses future: **voy a comer** (I'm going to eat), **vamos a salir** (we're going to leave).
**Al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction) whenever the destination has a masculine article.

**Venir** is irregular. Present tense: **vengo**, **vienes**, **viene**, **venimos**, **venís**, **vienen**.
**Ven a mi casa** — **Ven** is the informal (tú) imperative.
**Venir a + place** = to come to a place. Contrast: **ir** (to go, away from speaker) vs **venir** (to come, towards speaker) — same speaker-relative logic as English.

**Me encanta viajar** — **encantar** works like **gustar**: the subject is the thing loved (**viajar**), not the person. **Me** = indirect object (to me). Literally: 'travelling enchants me'.
Single thing/verb: **me encanta** · Multiple things: **me encantan los viajes**.
**Viajar** is a regular **-ar** verb: viajo, viajas, viaja, viajamos, viajáis, viajan.

**Camino al trabajo** — **al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction before masculine noun **trabajo**).
**Caminar** is a regular **-ar** verb. Also common: **andar** (to walk/go on foot) — more colloquial in Spain.
**Todos los días** = every day (literally 'all the days'). Compare: **cada día** (each day) — both are common and interchangeable.
cards.englishPhrase: I walk to work every day.
cards.phrase: Camino al trabajo todos los días.

cards.grammarNote:
**Manejar** = to drive (Latin America). In Spain: **conducir** (irregular: conduzco, conduces…).
**Maneja a la oficina** — **a la** because **oficina** is feminine. Compare masculine: **al trabajo** (to work), **al banco** (to the bank).
**Ella** (she) is optional — Spanish drops subject pronouns since verb endings identify the subject.

**Gira a la izquierda** — the three core direction commands:
**Gira a la derecha** (turn right) · **Gira a la izquierda** (turn left) · **Ve todo recto** (go straight ahead).
**Gira** is the informal (tú) imperative of **girar**. Formal (usted): **Gire a la izquierda**.
Alternative: **doblar** — **dobla a la derecha** is equally natural.
cards.phrase: Gira a la izquierda en la esquina.

cards.grammarNote:
**Cruza la calle** — **Cruza** is the informal (tú) imperative of **cruzar**. Formal: **Cruce la calle**.
**Con cuidado** = carefully (literally 'with care'). **Cuidado** is also used as a standalone warning: **¡Cuidado!** = Watch out!
**Cruzar** is a regular **-ar** verb: cruzo, cruzas, cruza… Note spelling change in preterite (yo): **crucé** (not *cruzé*).

**Sigue esta calle** — **Sigue** is the informal (tú) imperative of **seguir**. Formal: **Siga esta calle**.
**Seguir** is irregular: **sigo**, sigues, sigue, seguimos, seguís, siguen (yo form has irregular **g**).
**Seguir + gerund** = to keep doing something: **sigue recto** (keep going straight), **sigue caminando** (keep walking).

**Llegar a** = to arrive at / to reach. Always followed by **a**: **llegar a la estación**, **llegar al aeropuerto** (**al** = a + el).
**¿Cómo llego a...?** = How do I get to...? — one of the most useful travel questions.
**Llego** (yo form) is regular; note **llegué** in the preterite (spelling change: gu before é).
cards.englishPhrase: How do I reach the station?

cards.grammarNote:
**Entra por la puerta** — **entrar por** = to enter through. The preposition is **por** (through/by way of), not **en**.
**Entrar en** is also used (especially in Spain): **entra en el edificio** (enter the building). Latin American Spanish often omits **en**: **entra al edificio**.
**La puerta principal** — **principal** = main. Adjectives follow the noun in Spanish.

**Salir** is irregular in yo: **salgo** (I leave/go out). Other forms are regular: sales, sale, salimos, salís, salen.
**Salgo del trabajo** — **del** = **de + el** (mandatory contraction). You can never write *de el trabajo*.
**A las cinco** = at five (o'clock). Time expressions use **a las** (feminine plural) because **hora** (hour) is feminine: **a la una**, **a las dos**, **a las cinco**.
cards.phrase: Salgo del trabajo a las cinco.

cards.grammarNote:
**Partir** = to depart/leave (more formal or literary than **salir**). **El tren parte** = the train departs.
**A las nueve** = at nine. Times 2–12 use **a las**; only 1 o'clock uses **a la una**.
**El tren** is masculine — **el tren**, **un tren**. Transport nouns: **el avión** (plane), **el barco** (boat), **el autobús** (bus).
cards.phrase: El tren parte a las nueve.

cards.grammarNote:
**Llegar** = to arrive. Regular **-ar** verb (with a spelling change in preterite yo: **llegué**).
**Llegamos mañana** — **llegamos** is both present (we arrive) and preterite (we arrived) for nosotros; **mañana** (tomorrow) here confirms it's present/future.
**Llegar a** + place = to arrive at: **llegamos a Madrid**, **llegamos al hotel** (**al** = a + el).

**Quiero visitar el museo** — **querer + infinitive** expresses wanting to do something.
**Visitar** is a regular **-ar** verb: visito, visitas, visita, visitamos, visitáis, visitan.
**Visitar** takes a direct object with no preposition: **visitar el museo**, **visitar a un amigo** (personal 'a' before a person — **a** before a specific human direct object is mandatory in Spanish).
cards.englishPhrase: I want to visit the museum.

cards.grammarNote:
**En** covers both *in* and *at* for location: **en la bolsa** (in the bag), **en la oficina** (at the office), **en el aeropuerto** (at the airport).
**Las llaves están en la bolsa** — location uses **estar**. **Las llaves** (the keys) is feminine plural.
Contrast **en** (location/state) with **a** (direction/destination): **Estoy en casa** vs **Voy a casa**.
cards.englishPhrase: The keys are in the bag.
cards.phrase: Las llaves están en la bolsa.

cards.grammarNote:
**Sobre** = on top of / above / about. **El libro está sobre la mesa** = The book is on the table.
**Encima de** is a near-synonym meaning *on top of*: **encima de la mesa** — slightly more emphatic about vertical position.
**En** can also mean *on* in some contexts: **en la mesa** (on the table) is equally natural and very common.
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.
cards.phrase: El libro está sobre la mesa.

cards.grammarNote:
Spanish uses **en** for both *in* and *at* with locations: **Estoy en la oficina** = I'm at/in the office.
**Estoy** is from **estar** — always use **estar** (not **ser**) for location.
The key contrast: **en** (being somewhere) vs **a** (going somewhere): **Estoy en el banco** (I'm at the bank) vs **Voy al banco** (I'm going to the bank).

**Debajo de** = under/below. Always two words, always followed by **de**.
**Debajo de la mesa** — **de + la** stays as **de la** (no contraction needed; contractions only apply to **de + el**).
Compare position prepositions: **encima de** (on top of) · **debajo de** (under) · **al lado de** (next to) · **delante de** (in front of) · **detrás de** (behind).
cards.englishPhrase: The cat is under the table.
cards.phrase: El gato está debajo de la mesa.

cards.grammarNote:
**Al lado de** = next to / beside. Broken down: **al** (= **a + el**) + **lado** (side) + **de** (of).
**Al lado del café** — **del** = **de + el** (mandatory contraction). The phrase contains *two* contractions: **al** and **del**.
Other position prepositions follow the same **[prep] + de** pattern: **cerca de**, **lejos de**, **delante de**, **detrás de**, **encima de**, **debajo de**.
cards.englishPhrase: The bank is next to the cafe.
cards.phrase: El banco está al lado del café.

cards.grammarNote:
**Entre** = between (two things) or among (more than two). Unlike most position words, it does NOT need **de**: **entre la escuela y la biblioteca** (not *entre de*).
**El parque está entre...** — location uses **estar**.
**Y** = and. Before words starting with **i** or **hi**, **y** changes to **e**: **padre e hijo** (father and son).
cards.englishPhrase: The park is between the school and the library.
cards.phrase: El parque está entre la escuela y la biblioteca.

cards.grammarNote:
**En frente de** = in front of. Also written as **enfrente de** (one word). Both are correct.
**En frente del hotel** — **del** = **de + el** (mandatory contraction).
Compare: **delante de** (physically in front of, e.g., leading the way) vs **enfrente de** (facing/opposite, e.g., across the street).
cards.englishPhrase: The taxi is in front of the hotel.
cards.phrase: El taxi está en frente del hotel.

cards.grammarNote:
**Detrás de** = behind. Always followed by **de**.
**Detrás de la casa** — **de + la** = **de la** (no contraction; only **de + el** contracts to **del**).
Position preposition family: **delante de** (in front of) · **detrás de** (behind) · **encima de** (on top of) · **debajo de** (under) · **al lado de** (next to) · **enfrente de** (facing).
cards.englishPhrase: The garden is behind the house.
cards.phrase: El jardín está detrás de la casa.

cards.grammarNote:
**Cerca de** = near. **Cerca de aquí** = near here. When followed by a masculine noun: **cerca del banco** (del = de + el).
**¿Hay un restaurante cerca de aquí?** — **hay** (there is) introduces new/indefinite information. Compare: **¿Dónde está el restaurante?** (where is the [specific] restaurant — **está** for known/specific).
**Cerca** alone (no **de**) = nearby: **¿Hay una farmacia cerca?**
cards.englishPhrase: Is there a restaurant near here?

cards.grammarNote:
**Lejos de** = far from. Opposite of **cerca de** (near).
**¿Está lejos el aeropuerto?** — inverted word order for questions is natural in Spanish (subject after verb). **El aeropuerto** = **el** + masculine noun.
**El aeropuerto** — etymology: **aero** (air) + **puerto** (port). The same compound in English: *air + port*.

Three words for 'here': **aquí** (here, right at this spot — most common), **acá** (here, more general — common in Latin America), **aca** (informal variant).
**Ven aquí** — **Ven** is the irregular informal imperative of **venir**. Formal: **Venga aquí**.
**Por favor** = please (literally 'by/for favour'). Always a polite addition when making requests.

Three words for 'there': **aquí** (here) · **allí** (there, at a specific spot) · **allá** (over there, more vague/distant).
**El taxi está allí** — location uses **estar**.
**Allí mismo** = right there (very precise); **allá lejos** = way over there (distant). The distinction between **allí** and **allá** mirrors **aquí** vs **acá**.

**En todas partes** = everywhere (literally 'in all parts'). A fixed phrase — very natural in speech.
**Hay turistas en todas partes** — **hay** = there are (plural). **Hay** is invariable; it never changes regardless of number.
**Por todas partes** is an alternative meaning 'all over the place / throughout': **hay turistas por todas partes**.

**En algún lugar** = somewhere (literally 'in some place'). **Algún** is the shortened form of **alguno** before a masculine singular noun: **algún lugar**, **algún día**, **algún amigo**.
**Vamos a algún lugar tranquilo** — **vamos a** = let's go to. **Tranquilo** agrees with masculine **lugar**.
Alternative: **en algún sitio** (also very common, especially in Spain).

**En ningún lugar** = nowhere. **Ningún** is the shortened form of **ninguno** before a masculine singular noun (like **algún**).
**No hay ningún lugar** — Spanish uses double negation: **no** before the verb + **ningún** = nowhere/not any. This is grammatically correct and required (unlike in English where double negatives are avoided).
**Sentarse** is reflexive (to sit oneself down): **sentarse**, **me siento**, **te sientas**.
cards.phrase: No hay ningún lugar para sentarse.

cards.grammarNote:
**A la izquierda** = to the left. The full direction phrase always uses **a + la** because **izquierda** is feminine.
**Gira a la izquierda** (turn left) — one of the three essential direction commands alongside **a la derecha** (right) and **todo recto** (straight ahead).
Remember: **izquierda** and **derecha** are nouns (feminine), not adjectives. That's why they take an article: **a la izquierda**, not *a izquierda*.

**A la derecha** = to the right. Like **izquierda**, **derecha** is a feminine noun and always takes **a + la**.
**Gira a la derecha** (turn right) · **Gira a la izquierda** (turn left) · **Ve todo recto** (go straight).
**Derecho/a** (adjective) = straight: **todo recto** or **todo derecho**. Don't confuse **derecha** (direction: right) with **derecho** (straight ahead or the noun 'right/law').

**Todo recto** = straight ahead (literally 'all straight'). Both **todo recto** and **todo derecho** are used — **todo recto** is slightly more common in directions.
**Ve todo recto** — **Ve** is the irregular informal imperative of **ir** (to go). Formal: **Vaya todo recto**.
This completes the trio of essential direction phrases: **a la derecha** · **a la izquierda** · **todo recto**.

**Al norte** = to the north. The contraction **al** = **a + el** is used even with compass directions since they are treated as masculine nouns.
All four cardinal directions are masculine: **el norte**, **el sur**, **el este**, **el oeste**.
Compass direction phrases: **al norte** (to/in the north), **al sur** (south), **al este** (east), **al oeste** (west).
cards.englishPhrase: The city is to the north.

cards.grammarNote:
**Al sur** = to the south. **Al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction — **sur** is masculine).
**La playa está al sur** — location with **estar**. The beach is located (permanently, for this context) to the south.
Cardinal directions: **norte** · **sur** · **este** · **oeste** — all masculine, all use **al** when saying 'to the [direction]'.
cards.englishPhrase: The beach is to the south.

cards.grammarNote:
**El este** = the east (masculine). **Por el este** = through/via the east — **por** indicates movement through a direction.
**Sale por el este** — **salir por** = to come out through. The sun 'comes out' (**sale**) through the east.
Beware of homonyms: **este** (east, noun, no accent) · **este** (this, demonstrative adjective, no accent) · **éste** (this one, demonstrative pronoun — accent optional in modern Spanish).
cards.englishPhrase: The sun rises in the east.
cards.phrase: El sol sale por el este.

cards.grammarNote:
**El oeste** = the west (masculine). **En el oeste** = in the west.
**Se pone** — **ponerse** (to set, for the sun) is reflexive. The reflexive **se** signals an automatic/natural process. **El sol se pone** (the sun sets) · **el sol sale** (the sun rises).
**Poner** also means 'to put/place': context distinguishes. Reflexive **ponerse** = to put on (clothing) or to set (sun).
cards.englishPhrase: The sun sets in the west.
cards.phrase: El sol se pone en el oeste.

cards.grammarNote:
**Vivir** = to live. Regular **-ir** verb: **vivo**, vives, vive, vivimos, vivís, viven.
**Vivo cerca del centro** — **del** = **de + el** (mandatory contraction). **Cerca de** = near.
**Vivir en** = to live in/at a place. **Vivo en Madrid**, **vivo en un apartamento**, **vivo cerca del centro**.
cards.englishPhrase: I live near the city centre.

cards.grammarNote:
**Quedarse** = to stay/remain (reflexive). The phrase uses **hospedarse** instead — a more specific word meaning to lodge/stay as a guest.
**Nos hospedamos** — reflexive verb with **nos** (we). **Hospedarse** comes from Latin **hospes** (host/guest) — same root as **hospital**, **hotel**, **hostal**.
**Quedarse en** = to stay at: **me quedo en el hotel** (I'm staying at the hotel).

**Te espero** = I'll wait for you. **Esperar a alguien** = to wait for someone — the 'for' is built into the verb; no preposition needed before the person.
**Te** = you (indirect object pronoun, informal). Formal: **Le espero**.
**Esperar** also means *to hope*: **espero que llegues pronto** (I hope you arrive soon). Context distinguishes.

**Quedamos en el parque** uses **quedar** (to arrange to meet) — very natural in Spain for planned meetings.
**Quedar en** = to meet at (a place). Alternative: **encontrarse en**: **nos encontramos en el parque**.
**Quedar** has many meanings: to remain (**queda poco tiempo** = little time remains), to fit (**te queda bien** = it suits you), to arrange to meet (**quedamos a las tres** = let's meet at three).

**Perderse** is reflexive — the pronoun changes with the subject:
**me pierdo** · **te pierdes** · **se pierde**
Many movement/location verbs are reflexive: **quedarse** (to stay), **hospedarse** (to lodge), **dirigirse** (to head)
**¡No te pierdas!** = Don't get lost!

**No encuentro el hotel** — **encontrar** is a stem-changing verb (o→ue): **encuentro**, encuentras, encuentra, encontramos, encontráis, encuentran.
**No encuentro** = I can't find / I don't find. Spanish present tense covers the English 'can't find' in this context.
Contrast: **encontrar** (to find something) vs **encontrarse** (reflexive: to be located, or to meet someone).

**Busco la farmacia** — **buscar** means 'to look for'. The 'for' is already inside the verb — never add **por** or **para**: say **busco el hotel**, not *busco por el hotel*.
**Buscar** is a regular **-ar** verb (with spelling change in preterite yo: **busqué**).
**La farmacia** — feminine noun. Pharmacies in Spanish-speaking countries are marked by a green cross.

**Dirigirse** is reflexive — to head (oneself) towards. **Dirígete** is the informal (tú) reflexive imperative.
**Hacia** = towards. Distinguish from **a** (to/at): **hacia la estación** (in the direction of the station) vs **a la estación** (to the station — final destination).
**Dirigirse** is more formal; in everyday speech: **ve hacia la estación** or **camina hacia la estación**.

**Continúa todo recto** — **Continúa** is the informal (tú) imperative of **continuar**. Formal: **Continúe todo recto**.
**Todo recto** = straight ahead. Also: **sigue recto** (keep going straight) using **seguir**.
**Continuar + gerund** = to keep doing: **continúa caminando** (keep walking). **Seguir + gerund** is equally common: **sigue caminando**.

**Para en la esquina** — **Para** is the informal (tú) imperative of **parar** (to stop). Formal: **Pare en la esquina**.
**Parar** (transitive) = to stop something; **pararse** (reflexive) = to stop oneself/come to a stop.
Also common: **detenerse** = to stop (slightly more formal): **detente aquí** (stop here).

**Aparcar** (Spain) / **estacionar** (Latin America) = to park a vehicle. Both are correct.
**¿Dónde puedo aparcar?** — **poder + infinitive** = to be able to / can. **Puedo** is the yo form (stem-changing: o→ue): **puedo**, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden.
**Dónde** = where (with accent in questions). Without accent: **donde** = where (in relative clauses).

**Reservé una habitación** — preterite tense (past, completed action). **Reservé** = I reserved/booked (yo form of **reservar** — regular **-ar** preterite).
**Una habitación** = a room. Note: **la habitación** (hotel room) vs **el cuarto** (room in a house, more general) vs **la sala** (living room).
**Habitación** ends in **-ción** → feminine (all **-ción** nouns are feminine).

**Nos registramos** — reflexive verb **registrarse** (to register/check in). **Nos** = ourselves (nosotros reflexive pronoun).
**A las tres** = at three (o'clock). Times use **a + las** because **hora** is feminine: **a la una** (1:00), **a las dos** (2:00), **a las tres** (3:00).
**Llegada** = arrival (check-in). **Salida** = departure (check-out) — useful hotel vocabulary pair.

**El check-out es a las once** — **es** (not **está**) because this is a scheduled, defining characteristic (checkout time is defined as 11am).
**A las once** = at eleven. Only **a la una** (singular); all others use **a las**: a las dos, a las once.
**Hacer el check-out** is the direct borrowing. More Spanish alternatives: **hacer el check-out**, **dejar la habitación**, **registrar la salida**.
cards.phrase: El check-out es a las once.

cards.grammarNote:
**Cerca** (no **de**) = nearby (standalone adverb, no specific reference).
**Cerca de** + noun = near [something]: **cerca de aquí** (near here), **cerca del banco** (near the bank — **del** = de + el).
**¿Hay una farmacia cerca?** — **hay** (there is/are) introduces indefinite existence. If you already know a specific pharmacy: **¿Dónde está la farmacia?** (Where is the pharmacy?).

**El mercado está muy concurrido** — use **estar** (not **ser**) because crowdedness is a temporary state.
**Concurrido** agrees with the subject: **mercado concurrido** (m.) · **calle concurrida** (f.) · **lugares concurridos** (m.pl.) · **calles concurridas** (f.pl.).
Alternatives: **lleno** (full) · **animado** (lively) · **transitado** (busy with traffic).

**Prefiero un barrio tranquilo** — **tranquilo** = quiet/calm. It follows the noun it modifies (adjectives usually follow in Spanish).
**Tranquilo** agrees with the noun: **barrio tranquilo** (m.) · **calle tranquila** (f.) · **lugares tranquilos** (m.pl.).
**Prefiero** = I prefer (stem-changing verb e→ie): prefiero, prefieres, prefiere, preferimos, preferís, prefieren.

**Estoy perdido** — use **estar** (not **ser**) because being lost is a temporary state.
**Perdido** (m.) / **perdida** (f.) — the adjective agrees with the subject: **estoy perdido** (male speaker) · **estoy perdida** (female speaker).
Related: **perderse** (to get lost — reflexive): **me perdí** (I got lost) · **¡No te pierdas!** (Don't get lost!)
